claim: Giving agents unrestricted SQL read access to a centralized production database is extremely powerful because it allows agents to answer arbitrary business questions without requiring specialized data engineers.
actionable: When building agent systems, provide read-only SQL access to a comprehensive production database as a high-leverage tool rather than restricting agents to pre-built narrowly-scoped query functions.

claim: Jevons Paradox applies to agent tools: reducing friction to access data dramatically increases question frequency and complexity, not just ease of answer retrieval.
actionable: When removing friction from workflows with AI agents, expect exponentially increased demand rather than just improved efficiency of existing workflows.

claim: Large denormalized databases optimized for agent retrieval (GBrain-style) with RAG, graph RAG, and reranking produce more capable agents than traditional normalized schemas.
actionable: Denormalize data specifically for agent use cases, combining retrieval-augmented generation, graph structures, and reranking to maximize agent capability for complex queries.

claim: Multiplayer agent harnesses (team/organizational level) are more difficult to build than single-player agents, with key challenges including shared context layers and tool registry coordination.
actionable: When scaling agents to teams, invest in shared context layers and centralized tool registries as foundational infrastructure rather than treating agents as individual power tools.

claim: A shared internal tool registry (350+ tools at YC) makes agents useful at work because it enables agents to perform domain-specific work, not just answer questions.
actionable: Build comprehensive shared tool registries across your organization so agents can execute work (booking, managing, analyzing) rather than limiting them to information retrieval.

claim: Skills should follow DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) and MECE (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive) principles, and models intuitively understand these organizing principles when given a resolver table.
actionable: Organize agent skills using DRY and MECE principles with explicit resolver tables so models can optimize skill selection and avoid redundant tool calls.

claim: Self-improving skill loops where agents automatically read conversation transcripts, identify improvements, and update skills overnight are emerging as a key mechanism for organizational capability scaling.
actionable: Implement automated skill improvement loops where agents analyze transcripts of their own usage, identify shortcomings, and meta-prompt improvements nightly without human intervention.

claim: Recording and transcribing all agent conversations creates a shared organizational knowledge layer that enables collective skill improvement and context transfer across teams.
actionable: Make agent conversation recording and transcription a default practice to build organizational memory and enable automated skill improvement from aggregate usage patterns.

claim: Making agent conversations globally viewable to all employees, while counterintuitive for security, actually enables social control of access patterns and reduces need for formal security policies.
actionable: In high-trust environments, make agent interactions transparent and globally visible to reduce formal security constraints while maintaining behavioral alignment through social visibility.

claim: Agentic systems require egalitarian and trust-by-default organizational cultures to function effectively; these traits are rare in most existing organizations but essential for superintelligent organizations.
actionable: When building agentic infrastructure, assess and actively develop egalitarian and trust-default culture as foundational, not as afterthoughts to technical implementation.

claim: Chat is the optimal interface for agents because it most closely mirrors human language and thinking, allowing users to progressively trust agents with more autonomy without requiring custom UIs.
actionable: Build agentic systems with chat-first interfaces that enable natural language expression of intent and trust-building through interaction, rather than designing specialized GUIs.

claim: Just-in-time software generation by agents (creating single-page JS apps for specific tasks) will become the dominant paradigm instead of building monolithic applications upfront.
actionable: Design agent applications to dynamically generate task-specific software interfaces on-demand rather than building and maintaining large static codebases.

claim: Raising the floor: new employees ramp significantly faster with AI agent access to organizational knowledge and skill demonstrations, potentially reducing 6-month ramp to days.
actionable: Invest in agent infrastructure that lets new employees learn organizational processes from recorded demonstrations and interact with institutional knowledge to accelerate onboarding.

claim: Two-sentence pitches improved noticeably after agents were meta-prompted with transcripts of partners' feedback sessions, demonstrating how organizational knowledge can be systematically captured and applied.
actionable: Use meta-prompting with feedback transcripts to systematically improve agent skills rather than relying on one-off hand-written prompts.
